picks.name: Pearl Izumi Quest Bib Short
picks.blurb: Solid entry-level bib that won't punish your wallet or your sit bones. Great if you're new to long days and not ready to drop premium money on something you'll find out you love or hate.

picks.name: Castelli Endurance 3 Bib Shorts
picks.blurb: The reliable mid-tier workhorse. Castelli's pad is famously comfortable, and these hold up to repeat wash cycles all summer long.

picks.name: Rapha Pro Team Bib Shorts
picks.blurb: If you ride enough miles to know what you want, Rapha's Pro Team line is the move. Compressive, supportive, and the chamois holds up over multi-day rides.

picks.name: Giro Register MIPS
picks.blurb: A great-fitting, MIPS-equipped helmet for under a hundred bucks. There's no reason to skip MIPS in 2026.

picks.name: Specialized Align II MIPS
picks.blurb: Another sub-$100 pick that punches well above its price. Comfortable on long days, plenty of vents.

picks.name: Specialized S-Works Prevail 3
picks.blurb: The race helmet a lot of pros wear. Light, ridiculously well-vented, and looks the part. Worth it if you're racking up serious miles.

picks.name: Shimano RC3 Road Shoes
picks.blurb: Stiff sole, BOA dial, plays nicely with most pedal systems. A great first pair of clipless shoes.

picks.name: Shimano 105 SPD-SL Pedals (PD-R7000)
picks.blurb: The pedal that's been on more bikes than maybe any other. Reliable, serviceable, easy to clip in and out.

picks.name: Specialized S-Works Torch Road Shoes
picks.blurb: Stupid stiff, very light, dialed fit. If your feet have ever gone numb on a century, these are a real solution.

picks.name: Cygolite Metro Pro 1100 (front)
picks.blurb: Bright enough for early-morning WAM rollouts, with a beam pattern that doesn't blind oncoming riders. USB rechargeable.

picks.name: Cygolite Hotrod 90 (rear)
picks.blurb: A rear blinker that's actually visible in daylight. Tiny, light, lasts forever on a charge.

picks.name: Bontrager Flare RT (rear)
picks.blurb: The gold standard for daytime visibility. If we could only pick one safety upgrade, it's this light.

picks.name: Specialized Power Comp with MIMIC
picks.blurb: The cutout saddle a lot of TGT riders swear by. The MIMIC version adds soft-tissue support that makes a real difference on long days.

picks.name: Selle Italia SLR Boost Superflow
picks.blurb: Short-nose, pressure-relieving, and light. A good pick if you want a racy fit without giving up comfort.

picks.name: Brooks C17 Cambium
picks.blurb: Vulcanized rubber instead of leather, no break-in, surprisingly cushy on rough roads. The "I just want to be comfortable" pick.

picks.name: Camelbak Podium Chill 24oz
picks.blurb: Insulated, leak-resistant, and the bite valve doesn't dribble down your jersey. Bring two.

picks.name: Skratch Labs Sport Hydration Mix
picks.blurb: Tastes like real food (because it basically is). Dial in your bottles with this and you'll feel better at mile 80 than you do at mile 20 with sugar water.

picks.name: Maurten Gel 100
picks.blurb: The hydrogel gel a lot of endurance athletes use. Sits easy on the stomach, no sticky aftermath. Keep a few in your jersey pocket.

picks.name: Crankbrothers M17 Multi-Tool
picks.blurb: Seventeen tools, fits in your jersey pocket, covers basically every roadside fix you'll need.

picks.name: Lezyne Twin Speed Drive CO2 Inflator
picks.blurb: Compact, dual-threaded, threads onto the cartridge nice and clean. Pair it with a couple 16g cartridges and you're set.

picks.name: Topeak Mountain Morph Mini Pump
picks.blurb: A backup pump for when you blow through your CO2 or want to top off a tube without using a cartridge. Folds out into a mini floor pump, which is a small miracle.

picks.name: Wahoo Elemnt Bolt v2
picks.blurb: The bike computer half of TGT uses. Easy to set up, syncs with Strava, and the buttons work even when your hands are sweaty or gloved.

picks.name: Garmin Edge 540
picks.blurb: The other half of TGT uses this one. More mapping power, longer battery, deep ecosystem if you're already in Garmin's world.

picks.name: JBL Clip 5 Bluetooth Speaker
picks.blurb: Small, loud enough, clips to a saddle bag or backpack strap. The TGT speaker situation is non-negotiable; pick one.

picks.name: Therm-a-Rest NeoAir XLite NXT
picks.blurb: The pad most experienced bike-tourers carry. Light, packs small, genuinely comfortable. Worth every dollar after day one.

picks.name: REI Co-op Camp Bed 3.5
picks.blurb: If you're sleeping in a high school gym (which most WAM riders do), a thicker self-inflating pad beats an ultralight one. Comfort > grams when you're not carrying it on the bike.

picks.name: Big Agnes Copper Spur HV UL2
picks.blurb: If you're tenting it, this is the standard. Two-person, freestanding, easy to pitch in a parking lot or a field. Holds up.
